preFix.title: Broad-edge capital flourishes
preFix.subtitle: Adding swash entries, extended exits, and decorative loops to foundational and italic capital letters
preFix.excerpt: Flourishes on broad-edge capitals use the pen's natural thick-and-thin to create extended entry strokes, looped serifs, and sweeping exit arcs. Unlike pointed-pen copperplate flourishing, which uses hairlines and pressure shades, broad-edge flourishes are built on stroke-width contrast, thick main strokes, thin crossing hairlines, and are drawn at nib angle rather than free-form.
preFix.sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906), chapter on formal capitals and free capitals. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Johnston discusses the distinction between canonical letter forms and their flourished extensions.

content.text: is a stroke, an entry, an exit, or an internal extension, that uses the same

as the rest of the letter but travels further or in a more curved path. Because the nib angle is maintained, the thick-and-thin logic of the letter continues into the flourish without looking grafted on. A swash that changes angle or becomes a brushstroke rather than a pen stroke reads as an error, not a decoration.

An extended entry curves before meeting the main body of the letter, the A, for example, begins with a sweeping curved stroke from the left that rises to the apex rather than starting immediately at the apex. Keep the curve smooth and wide; tight entries look cramped. The entry stroke is thin at the beginning (coming from a near-vertical direction) and thickens as it meets the main stroke.

A loop serif replaces the standard serif at the foot or head of a capital stem. The pen exits the stem, makes a small loop, and re-enters. The loop must be drawn with the pen at the same angle as the letter, a loop that changes angle produces an ugly spike rather than a graceful curl.

draws a fine diagonal through the body of a letter or across the exit arc. On the D, for example, a hairline from the cap height to just below the baseline crossing the curved back gives the letter a sense of forward lean. Draw the hairline in a single motion from the cap height downward; stop it below the baseline rather than ending at it.

A, C, D, E, G, H, K, N, Q, R, T, W, and Y take entry or exit extensions naturally. B, F, I, J, L, M, P, S, U, V, X, and Z are harder, their structures leave fewer natural extension points and most attempts look forced. In a composition, flourish only the first capital letter and occasional display letters; flourishing every capital makes a text illegible.

items.fix: Plan and lightly pencil the flourish before writing the letter. Then write the letter and its extension as one connected action.
items.cause: The flourish was drawn as a separate stroke rather than as a continuation of the letter construction.
items.symptom: The flourishes look like afterthoughts added to ordinary letters

items.fix: Practice the entry curve in isolation as a repeated drill. Write ten entry curves of the same letter before adding the body. Consistency comes from repetition, not from careful attention to each individual stroke.
items.cause: The entry is drawn freehand at different sizes each time.
items.symptom: The looped entry strokes are inconsistent, some tight, some wide

initialFindings.message: TECHNIQUE tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
paragraph reads at grade 11.1 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
